auth.mfaRecovery.explainReEnroll: Recovery codes can't stand in for your authenticator long term, so this session runs at reduced assurance. Set up a new authenticator now (or remove the lost one) to restore full two-factor protection and generate fresh recovery codes.
auth.mfaRecovery.skipHint: If you still have your authenticator, you can also just remove and re-add it from the same screen.

marketing.waitlist.successBody: We'll email you when ${productName} opens. No drip campaign, just the one note that matters.
marketing.waitlist.fields.name: Name (optional)
marketing.pages.ai.auroraLink.title: The assistant behind every draft has a name.
marketing.pages.ai.auroraLink.body: Every use case on this page runs on Aurora, the one assistant across the whole ecosystem. See where it works today, what is in preview, and what ships next.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.metadata.tit: ATLVS: The Operator Console, Coming Soon
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.metadata.des: ATLVS is the operator console we're building now: ERP, CRM, and project management on one record store, with the Sell to Settle spine and the Coordinate Matrix task lens. Join the waitlist.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.pillars.spin: Every production will run the same eight-phase arc, from the first pitch to the final settlement. The console will show you exactly which phase you're in and what has to be true before the next gate opens.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.pillars.matr: Work plotted as phase by department: 90 coordinates that tell you what production owes design in week three, and what finance owes everyone at close. You'll see the whole build on one grid.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.pillars.adva: One packet out per counterparty, one structured submission back, against a real deadline. Crew lists, travel grids, rider uploads. The chase ladder will run itself so you don't have to.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.pillars.reco: Finance and procurement on one record store
Proposals, budgets, purchase orders, invoices, and expenses will live on the same records as the work itself. When the show settles, the numbers are already where they need to be.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.faq.what.a: ATLVS is the operator console of the ATLVS Technologies ecosystem: ERP, CRM, and project management for experiential production, built on one shared record store. It covers the full arc from selling the work to settling it.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.faq.when.a: We haven't set a date, and we won't announce one until we're sure of it. The waitlist is the first place we'll say so. In the meantime COMPVSS, the field operations app, is live today.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.faq.waitlist: What does joining the waitlist get me?
One email when ATLVS opens, and a spot near the front of the line for early access. We don't run drip campaigns and we don't share the list with anyone.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.hero.title: The Operator Console Is On The Bench
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.pillarsSecti: ERP, CRM, and PM on one record store
One console where the proposal, the build, the crew, and the money share the same records.
Aurora AI, powered by Brio, rides along in the console: an assistant grounded in your own projects, crew, and budgets, never in another org's data.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.honest.title: Being built now. No fake launch date.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.honest.body: ATLVS is in active development on the same platform that already runs COMPVSS in the field. We'd rather ship it right than ship it loud, so the only date we'll ever give you is a real one. Waitlist members hear first.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.waitlist.sub: One email when it opens. That's the whole deal.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.cta.title: Meanwhile, The Ecosystem Is Open.
marketing.pages.atlvsTeaser.cta.subtitle: COMPVSS runs crews in the field today, and the marketplace is live. Start there and ATLVS will meet you where your records already are.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.meta.titl: Foundations · ATLVS Technologies — Brand & UI Kit
marketing.brandKit.foundations.meta.desc: ATLVS Technologies brand foundations: Waypoint mark, color, typography (MONUMENT — Anton + Hanken Grotesk), spacing, radii, elevation, motion, and iconography.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.logo.head: The Waypoint
marketing.brandKit.foundations.logo.lead: — an eight-point navigational star with a center void, the navigation system rendered as one geometric glyph. Neutral SaaS register, recolored per product. It is distinct from the cosmic GHXSTSHIP ghost-ship skull, which is the parent-company mark only.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.logo.note: The tile carries the product accent; the star stays white (or ink on light grounds). The wordmark is always spaced caps and nowrap — never lowercase, never unspaced except in URLs and IDs.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.logo.spec: — geometric, logo lockup only. Headings use Anton; body & UI use Hanken Grotesk.
, the V flipped. A and V read as mirror forms.
Spaced caps, nowrap. In the full lockup,
is letter-spaced to the exact ATLVS width.
Each product is the same waypoint on its own accent tile — instantly a family, instantly distinct.
Min-size — the mark holds at every scale
marketing.brandKit.foundations.clearspac: Keep margin equal to half the mark height on every side.
Recolor the tile to the product accent; keep the star white (or ink on light).
Use the bare mark at ≤16px; the tiled icon at ≥32px.
Recolor or gradient the star itself — only the tile carries color.
Rotate, stretch, or add the cosmic halftone or skull.
Set the wordmark unspaced or lowercase.
Place the bare mark on a busy photo without the solid tile.
The GHXSTSHIP skull appears only in the endorsement lockup — “an ATLVS Technologies, a GHXSTSHIP Industries company.” It is never the product app icon.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.color.lea: Neutral surfaces do the work; one accent per product carries identity. Every value resolves from the
namespace and re-tunes per mode for AA contrast.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.color.pro: Product identity — the accent each app owns
marketing.brandKit.foundations.type.head: Typography
marketing.brandKit.foundations.type.lead: Headings and metrics are set in
Anton, an ultra-condensed all-caps display face
— a single black weight that gives the product poster-scale presence. Body and UI stay
so tables and forms read clean;
is the eyebrow and coordinate vernacular (IDs, codes, labels); and
is the constructed wordmark lockup only. Display headings are ALL-CAPS (Anton is a caps face); body and labels stay Title / sentence case.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.type.spec: The default reading size for tables, forms, and interface copy.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.type.head: Header treatments
Three sanctioned ways to deploy the wide headline. Use the right one for the context — don't mix more than one per view.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.type.a2.n: Accent bar + live mono kicker. The default for app chrome and page headers.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.type.a3.n: One word takes the product accent — headlines self-brand. Covers, marketing, splash.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.type.a4.n: Tabular figures at 900 / expanded. Dashboards and reports.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.spacing.l: A 4px base grid. Density swaps the control and row rhythm — the same tokens tighten under
marketing.brandKit.foundations.radii.lea: Soft, productivity-tool friendly — never pop-art sharp. Pills for chips and toggles only.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.elevation: A cool, tight three-step scale: resting → hover → popover & modal. Shadows are subtle in light and lean on borders in dark.
marketing.brandKit.foundations.motion.le: Quick and unfussy. One easing curve, short durations, and a hard rule: never transition a token-backed
across a theme switch — recolor instantly and animate
marketing.brandKit.foundations.icons.lea: for status seals and emphasis. Line up with text at 16–20px; standalone actions at 24px. Single-color, inheriting
marketing.logoKit.meta.title: ATLVS Logo Kit · The Waypoint
marketing.logoKit.meta.description: The ATLVS mark is the Waypoint — an 8-point navigational star with a centered void. Primary lockup, per-product app-icons, clearspace, endorsement, do/don't.
marketing.logoKit.title: The Logo Kit
marketing.logoKit.intro: The ATLVS mark is a waypoint — an eight-point navigational star with a center void, the navigation system rendered as one geometric glyph. Neutral SaaS register; recolors per product. Distinct from the cosmic GHXSTSHIP ghost-ship skull — the parent-company mark.
marketing.logoKit.appIcons: Product app-icons — one mark, three accents
marketing.logoKit.favicon: Favicon / Min-size — the mark holds at every scale
marketing.logoKit.clearspace: Clearspace — keep margin = ½ mark height on all sides
marketing.logoKit.do1: Recolor the tile to the product accent; keep the mark white — or ink on light.
marketing.logoKit.do3: Use the bare mark at ≤16px; the tiled icon at ≥32px.
marketing.logoKit.dont1: Recolor or gradient the star itself — only the tile carries color.
marketing.logoKit.dont2: Rotate, stretch, or add the cosmic halftone/skull to it.
marketing.logoKit.dont3: Set the wordmark unspaced or lowercase.
marketing.logoKit.dont4: Place the mark on a busy photo without the solid tile.

marketing.pages.contact.form.walkthrough: I'd like a walkthrough of the platform for my team.
marketing.pages.customers.landingSoon.me: Verified numbers publish with the story
marketing.pages.developers.metadata.titl: Developers: Build On The Record Store
marketing.pages.developers.metadata.desc: An OpenAPI-described REST API with scoped personal access tokens: documents, reports, and advancing. Submit an integration, join the partner program, or read the spec.
marketing.pages.developers.domains.docum: 29 document types with a machine-readable contract each: JSON Schema, sample data, and merge-field paths. Post your data or a record id and get rendered HTML back.
marketing.pages.developers.domains.repor: A catalog of 77 KPI definitions and 43 reports, computed live from the caller's org data. Metrics without backing data return null.
marketing.pages.developers.domains.advan: Read a project's advance packet graph or create the packet shell, and track a merge send's per-recipient delivery funnel. Portal tokens never leave the platform.
marketing.pages.developers.scopes.docume: List document types and read each type's JSON Schema, sample, and merge-field paths.
Generate documents from your own data or from a record you can access.
marketing.pages.developers.scopes.report: Read the metric catalog, resolve metric values, and pull reports and snapshots.
marketing.pages.developers.scopes.advanc: Read advance packets, sections, audiences, and send-batch tracking boards.
Create a project's advance packet shell (manager role or above).
marketing.pages.developers.faq.live.a: Yes. The documents, reports, and advancing endpoints are in production and described in the OpenAPI spec at /api/v1/openapi.json. If it's in the spec, it's real; we don't document endpoints that don't exist.
marketing.pages.developers.faq.token.q: How do I get a token?
marketing.pages.developers.faq.token.a: Sign up, then mint a personal access token in your API settings. Tokens carry only the scopes you grant them, and a typo'd scope is rejected at mint time instead of failing silently later.
marketing.pages.developers.faq.sdk.a: Not yet. The API is plain REST over HTTPS with an OpenAPI 3 spec, so generated clients work today. When we ship an official SDK we'll say so here first.
marketing.pages.developers.hero.title: Build On The Record Store
marketing.pages.developers.hero.subtitle: Every production on the platform lives on one set of records: projects, documents, money, people. The API reads and writes those same records, scoped to exactly what you grant it.
marketing.pages.developers.apiSection.ti: What's Open To Third Parties Today
marketing.pages.developers.apiSection.su: Three domains are open to third parties now. Everything below ships in the spec and runs in production.
marketing.pages.developers.scopesSection: Tokens Carry Only What You Grant
Personal access tokens carry named scopes. A reporting integration gets reports:read and nothing else. These are the scopes open to third parties today.
Stripe is wired into the platform natively: checkout, Connect onboarding, and signature-verified webhook receipt. You don't build payment plumbing to integrate with us.
Built something on the API? Submit it for review and we'll list it in the integrations directory where operators go looking.
Agencies and implementation shops get a public profile in the live agency directory and a direct line to the team.
marketing.pages.developers.docsSection.s: OpenAPI 3 spec, generated from the live routes
marketing.pages.developers.docsSection.t: Mint a personal access token in API settings
marketing.pages.developers.cta.title: The Spec Is The Pitch.
marketing.pages.developers.cta.subtitle: Read it, mint a token, and make your first call this afternoon. When it works, list it.

marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.metadata.t: GVTEWAY: The Public Interface, Coming Soon
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.metadata.d: GVTEWAY is the public side of the ecosystem: ticketing, stakeholder portals, and the marketplace. The marketplace is open today; ticketing and portals are coming. Join the waitlist.
Open roles and gig postings from real productions, browsable without an account.
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.live.talen: Public talent EPKs and crew profiles, with open calls you can submit to today.
Published RFQs from buying orgs and public vendor profiles ready to take a bid.
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.coming.tic: Tickets will live on the same record store as the production itself: tiers, zones, gate scans, and the guest's whole night on one record. Fast at the gate, honest in the counts.
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.coming.por: Artists, vendors, sponsors, media, and delegations will each get their own way in: documents, advances, schedules, and a direct line to their account manager. No forwarded PDFs.
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.faq.what.a: GVTEWAY is the public interface of the ATLVS Technologies ecosystem: ticketing, stakeholder portals, and the marketplace. It's the side of the platform that guests, talent, vendors, and partners touch.
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.faq.live.q: What can I use right now?
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.faq.live.a: The marketplace is open today: gigs, open calls, talent EPKs, crew profiles, vendor directories, and published RFQs. Ticketing and the stakeholder portals are the parts still in the shop.
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.faq.when.q: When do ticketing and portals launch?
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.faq.when.a: There is no launch date to announce, and we won't invent one. Waitlist members get the news first, and the marketplace keeps growing in the meantime.
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.hero.title: Every World Needs A Way In
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.liveSectio: This isn't a mockup. The first GVTEWAY surface has been live for a while: real postings, real profiles, real RFQs.
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.comingSect: The rest of GVTEWAY is in active development. No launch date until we have a real one.
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.waitlist.s: One email when ticketing and portals open. Nothing else.
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.cta.title: Don't Wait At The Gate.
marketing.pages.gvtewayTeaser.cta.subtit: The marketplace is live and free to browse. Post a gig, publish an RFQ, or put your EPK where buyers already look.

marketing.integrations.partners.meta.des: Third-party integrations built by ATLVS partners. Verified Partner integrations pass our technical review; Certified integrations also pass end-to-end QA.
marketing.integrations.partners.title: Built By Partners. Verified By Us.
marketing.integrations.partners.lead: Third-party integrations that hit the ATLVS REST + GraphQL surface. Verified Partner integrations pass our technical review; Certified integrations also pass end-to-end QA on a live tenant. Build your own —
marketing.integrations.partners.emptyBef: No partner integrations are listed publicly yet.
marketing.integrations.partners.cta.brin: Open API + open partner program. No revenue share, no gatekeeping.
marketing.integrations.submit.meta.title: Submit a Partner Integration — ATLVS Partner Program
marketing.integrations.submit.meta.descr: Build an integration against the ATLVS REST + GraphQL API and we'll list it in our partner directory. Verified Partner reviews pass our tech checklist; Certified passes end-to-end QA on a live tenant.
marketing.integrations.submit.title: Build It. Ship It. We'll List It.
marketing.integrations.submit.lead: Once submitted, your proposal lands in the ATLVS partner-AM queue. We typically respond within 5 business days. Verified Partner review passes our technical checklist; Certified additionally passes end-to-end QA on a live tenant.
marketing.integrations.submit.placeholde: Field-report capture with auto-sync to ATLVS daily logs.
What it does, who it’s for, how it integrates with ATLVS.

marketing.integrations.thanks.body.befor: Your proposal is in the queue. The ATLVS partner-AM team typically responds within 5 business days. Watch the inbox you submitted with — we'll reach out from
marketing.legal.dpa.title: Data Processing Addendum
marketing.legal.dpa.intro: Our DPA incorporates the EU SCCs and UK IDTA. It auto-applies when you subscribe. Request a counter-signed copy from Settings → Compliance.
marketing.legal.dpa.roles: Roles: you are the Controller; we are the Processor.
marketing.legal.dpa.breach: Breach notification: within 48 hours of confirmation.
marketing.legal.dpa.transfers: Data transfers: SCCs + UK IDTA where applicable.
marketing.legal.privacy.title: Privacy Policy
marketing.legal.privacy.collect.heading: What We Collect
marketing.legal.privacy.collect.body: Account info, workspace content you upload, and operational telemetry (pageviews, errors, API timing).
marketing.legal.privacy.use.heading: How We Use It
marketing.legal.privacy.use.body: To run the Service, prevent abuse, and improve reliability. We do not sell customer data.
marketing.legal.privacy.subprocessors.bo: Supabase (hosting + database), Stripe (billing), Anthropic (AI), Vercel (edge + CDN).
marketing.legal.privacy.retention.body: Default 90 days for audit logs; customer content retained until you delete it or your org is deleted.
marketing.legal.sla.title: Service Level Agreement
marketing.legal.sla.uptime.after: monthly uptime. Scheduled maintenance windows are excluded.
marketing.legal.terms.title: Terms of Service
marketing.legal.terms.acceptance.heading: 1. Acceptance of Terms
marketing.legal.terms.acceptance.body: By accessing or using the ATLVS Technologies platform you agree to these Terms. If you do not agree, do not use the Service.
marketing.legal.terms.accounts.heading: 2. Accounts
marketing.legal.terms.accounts.body: You are responsible for safeguarding the credentials used to access your account and for any activity under your account.
marketing.legal.terms.billing.heading: 3. Subscription & Billing
marketing.legal.terms.billing.body: Paid plans are billed via Stripe. Taxes, cancellation, and refund policies are documented in your in-app billing settings.
marketing.legal.terms.data.heading: 4. Data Ownership
marketing.legal.terms.data.body: You own the content you upload. We process it solely to provide the Service. Export at any time from Settings → Compliance.
marketing.legal.terms.termination.body: Either party may terminate at any time. Your data remains exportable for 30 days post-termination.

marketing.pages.pricing.billingFaq.q1.ti: Can I change plans at any time?
marketing.pages.pricing.billingFaq.q1.co: Yes. Upgrade or downgrade whenever you like — changes are prorated to the day, and your data carries across every tier.

marketing.tools.capacity.meta.title: Venue Capacity Calculator: Maximum Occupancy by Use
marketing.tools.capacity.meta.descriptio: Estimate maximum legal occupancy from square footage and use type. IBC-aligned defaults for assembly, concentrated, standing, and dining.
marketing.tools.capacity.title: Venue Capacity Calculator.
marketing.tools.capacity.schemaDescripti: Estimate maximum venue occupancy from square footage and use type.
marketing.tools.capacity.lead: Estimate maximum legal occupancy from square footage and use type. IBC-aligned defaults for assembly, concentrated, standing, and dining configurations. Confirm with the AHJ before publishing.
marketing.tools.capacity.how.heading: How Occupant Load Works.
marketing.tools.capacity.how.body1: Occupant load is the maximum number of people permitted in a space, set by the International Building Code (IBC) and adopted (with modifications) by most US jurisdictions. The IBC sets per-occupant area factors by use type: 7 sq ft per person standing, 15 sq ft for unconcentrated assembly with tables, 5 sq ft for waiting areas. Multiply usable floor area by the appropriate factor (and divide) to land on a load.
marketing.tools.capacity.how.body2: Real-world capacity is the lower of the IBC load and what the venue's egress capacity supports: exit width, fixed seating layout, sprinkler coverage, and AHJ judgement all play in. Use this calculator as a starting estimate; confirm with the local Authority Having Jurisdiction before publishing capacity.
marketing.tools.capacity.cta.title: Capacity Lives In ATLVS.
marketing.tools.capacity.cta.subtitle: Per-venue capacity, ticket allocations, and gate-scan limits in one record.
marketing.tools.crewSize.meta.title: Crew Size Calculator: Security, Stewards, Medical
marketing.tools.crewSize.meta.descriptio: Estimate security headcount, stewards, and medical staffing tiers from attendance and event type. Honest rule-of-thumb ratios, labeled as such.
marketing.tools.crewSize.title: Crew Size Calculator.
marketing.tools.crewSize.faq.how.q: How is the crew size calculated?
marketing.tools.crewSize.faq.how.a: Security starts from a per-attendee ratio that slides by event risk profile (1 per 100 attendees for a concert, 1 per 75 for a festival, 1 per 250 for a corporate event, 1 per 125 for sports), then adds 2 per door lane and 1 roamer per 3 bar or concession points. Stewards use a lighter per-attendee ratio, and medical staffing steps through tiers (first-aid point, first-aid post, medical post, field medical unit) at roughly 2 first aiders per 1,000 attendees. Every ratio is shown inline next to its result.
marketing.tools.crewSize.faq.compliant.a: No. It is a planning aid, not engineering or AHJ advice. Legally required staffing comes from your event risk assessment, the venue license, local ordinance, and the authority having jurisdiction. Use this to shape the first budget and then verify with your security and medical providers.
marketing.tools.crewSize.faq.source.q: Where do the ratios come from?
marketing.tools.crewSize.faq.source.a: They are industry rules of thumb, the same 1:100 security baseline event operators use as a first pass, adjusted by risk profile. Every figure is labeled MODELED because none of them is a verbatim requirement from a statute or code. The parameterization mirrors how the XPMS compliance engine models staffing rules.
marketing.tools.crewSize.schemaDescripti: Estimate security, steward, and medical staffing from attendance and event type.
marketing.tools.crewSize.lead: Estimate security headcount, stewards, and medical staffing tiers from attendance and event type. Every ratio is a labeled rule of thumb, so you know exactly what you are looking at.
marketing.tools.fromXpms: From the XPMS compliance engine: the same rulebase that runs XMCE checks in LEG3ND.
marketing.tools.crewSize.how.heading: How staffing ratios work.
marketing.tools.crewSize.how.body1: There is no single statutory security ratio for events in the US. Operators start from a baseline near 1 security per 100 attendees and slide it by risk profile: alcohol service, standing crowds, multi-stage sites, and rival-crowd dynamics push it tighter; seated, credentialed corporate rooms relax it. Doors and bars get their own coverage on top, because those are the pinch points where incidents start.
marketing.tools.crewSize.how.body2: Medical staffing steps through tiers rather than a straight line: a first-aid point for small rooms, a staffed post with an ambulance on standby in the low thousands, and a physician-led field medical unit at festival scale. Your medical provider and the AHJ set the real numbers from the event medical plan; this tool tells you which conversation you are about to have.
marketing.tools.crewSize.cta.title: Staff It In COMPVSS.
marketing.tools.crewSize.cta.subtitle: Rosters, shifts, and day-of check-ins for the crew this calculator just sized.
marketing.tools.generator.meta.title: Generator Size Calculator: kVA for Event Power
marketing.tools.generator.meta.descripti: Size a generator from connected load, power factor, diversity, and headroom. kVA required, next standard rental size up, and a fuel estimate.
marketing.tools.generator.title: Generator Size Calculator.
marketing.tools.generator.faq.how.q: How is the generator size calculated?
marketing.tools.generator.faq.how.a: Connected load (entered in kW, or computed from amps and voltage) is multiplied by the demand or diversity factor to get running demand, divided by the power factor to convert to kVA, then padded by your headroom percentage. The suggested unit is the next size up from a list of standard rental genset ratings. The fuel line is a modeled approximation near 0.07 gallons of diesel per kWh at working loads.
No. It is a planning aid, not engineering or AHJ advice, and we are not your engineer of record. Temporary power design, feeder sizing, grounding, and permitting must be done by a licensed electrician or the engineer of record and accepted by the authority having jurisdiction.
marketing.tools.generator.faq.source.q: Where do the defaults come from?
marketing.tools.generator.faq.source.a: The 0.8 power factor is the standard genset rating convention, and the diversity-factor concept mirrors standard electrical load-calculation practice. The 20 percent headroom default and the fuel curve are labeled MODELED because they are rules of thumb, not code values. The basis for each figure is stated next to the input it drives, the same way the XPMS compliance engine labels its rule sources.
marketing.tools.generator.schemaDescript: Size an event generator from connected load, power factor, diversity, and headroom.
marketing.tools.generator.lead: Turn a load schedule into a kVA number, the next standard rental size up, and a fuel estimate. A planning aid for the power conversation, not a substitute for the engineer of record.
marketing.tools.generator.how.heading: How generator sizing works.
marketing.tools.generator.how.body1: Generators are rated in kVA at a stated power factor, usually 0.8. Your load schedule is in kW, so the conversion runs through two factors: diversity (not everything draws at once, so running demand is a fraction of connected load) and power factor (real power over apparent power). Add headroom for motor inrush and the gear that shows up on the truck unannounced, and you have the kVA the rental order needs.
marketing.tools.generator.how.body2: Oversizing has a cost beyond the rental line: a diesel set loafing under about 30 percent load wet stacks, fouling injectors and stacks over a multi-day run. Power vendors solve it with paralleled smaller units or load banks. Bring the demand number, the show schedule, and the redundancy requirement to the vendor and the engineer of record; they design from there.
marketing.tools.generator.cta.title: Power Runs Through COMPVSS.
marketing.tools.generator.cta.subtitle: Fuel checks, generator rounds, and site-ops tasks live in the field app your crew already carries.

marketing.tools.perDiem.meta.title: Per-Diem Calculator: Crew & Talent Travel Allowance
marketing.tools.perDiem.meta.description: Compute total per-diem for crew or talent across multiple cities and days. GSA-aligned default rates with custom rate overrides.
marketing.tools.perDiem.title: Per-Diem Calculator.
marketing.tools.perDiem.schemaDescriptio: Per-diem total calculator for production crew and talent.
marketing.tools.perDiem.lead: Compute total per-diem for crew or talent across multiple cities and days. GSA-aligned default rates; overridable per-city.
marketing.tools.perDiem.how.heading: How Per-Diem Works.
marketing.tools.perDiem.how.body1: Per-diem is the daily allowance paid to a traveler (typically crew, talent, or contractors) for meals and incidentals while away from their home base. The US General Services Administration (GSA) publishes city-by-city per-diem rates for federal travel; most production budgets use these as a defensible baseline. International rates come from the State Department's DSSR tables.
marketing.tools.perDiem.how.body2: Per-diem typically settles on a per-show or weekly cadence in cash or paystub. Tour managers track per-diem against the city × day rate matrix to avoid drift between projected and actual.
marketing.tools.perDiem.cta.title: Skip The Math.
marketing.tools.perDiem.cta.subtitle: Per-diem auto-calculates on every show in ATLVS, tied to your roster.
marketing.tools.restroom.meta.title: Restroom & ADA Calculator: Portable Units for Events
marketing.tools.restroom.meta.descriptio: Estimate portable restroom units, ADA-accessible counts (2010 ADA 5 percent rule), and handwash stations from attendance, duration, and alcohol service.
marketing.tools.restroom.title: Restroom & ADA Calculator.
marketing.tools.restroom.faq.how.q: How is the restroom count calculated?
marketing.tools.restroom.faq.how.a: The base is the standard industry sizing table: about 1 portable unit per 100 attendees for a 4-hour event, scaled up to 2x for events running 10 or more hours, plus 20 percent when alcohol is served and 10 percent when the crowd skews majority women. The ADA-accessible count is 5 percent of total units with a minimum of 1 per cluster, per the 2010 ADA Standards section 213. Handwash stations run 1 per 10 units.
marketing.tools.restroom.faq.compliant.a: No. It is a planning aid, not engineering or AHJ advice. The permit count is set by your local health department and the authority having jurisdiction, and permanent-venue fixture counts follow the adopted plumbing code. Use this to size the rental order conversation, then confirm with the permitting authority.
marketing.tools.restroom.faq.source.q: Where do the numbers come from?
marketing.tools.restroom.faq.source.a: The accessible count is derived from the 2010 ADA Standards section 213 (5 percent of units, minimum 1 per cluster). The fixture-count methodology traces to IPC 403.1, the plumbing-code table for permanent occupancies. The portable-unit base table, the alcohol uplift, and the handwash ratio are industry rules of thumb and are labeled MODELED, matching how the XPMS compliance engine separates derived rules from modeled ones.
marketing.tools.restroom.schemaDescripti: Estimate portable restroom units, ADA-accessible counts, and handwash stations for events.
marketing.tools.restroom.lead: Estimate portable units, ADA-accessible counts, and handwash stations from attendance, duration, and alcohol service. The accessible math follows the 2010 ADA Standards; the sizing table is honest about being a rule of thumb.
marketing.tools.restroom.how.heading: How sanitation counts work.
marketing.tools.restroom.how.body1: Permanent venues size restrooms from the plumbing code: IPC 403.1 sets minimum fixture counts per occupant by occupancy type. Outdoor and temporary events do not get a single national table, so the industry converged on a sizing practice keyed to attendance, event length, and alcohol service, and your local health department turns that into a permit condition.
marketing.tools.restroom.how.body2: Accessibility is the part with hard numbers: the 2010 ADA Standards section 213 requires at least 5 percent of units to be accessible, with a minimum of 1, and the practical reading for events is at least 1 accessible unit in every cluster so nobody crosses the site to find one. Place them on firm, level routes and keep the doors clear of cable ramps.
marketing.tools.restroom.cta.title: Site Ops Live In COMPVSS.
marketing.tools.restroom.cta.subtitle: Service rounds, cluster checks, and vendor punch lists in the field app your ops crew carries.

m.emergency.title: Emergency Card
m.emergency.codesHint: Tap a code for the department, team and individual plan.
m.emergency.code.red.trigger: Fire, Lightning Strike, High Winds or Weather
m.emergency.code.red.dept: Halt all activity; initiate the evacuation sequence per zone marshals.
m.emergency.code.red.team: Open and hold your egress lanes; suppress re-entry.
m.emergency.code.red.indiv: Direct patrons to the nearest exit, sweep your sector, report headcount to your lead.
m.emergency.code.orange.dept: Pause the stage program; activate the crowd-density protocol.
m.emergency.code.orange.team: Form a relief lane; ease pressure toward your gate.
m.emergency.code.orange.indiv: Hold the barricade line, call the surge level on your radio channel, assist anyone down.
m.emergency.code.yellow.trigger: Structural Damage or Severe Equipment Failure
m.emergency.code.yellow.dept: Cordon the affected structure; dispatch rigging and engineering.
m.emergency.code.yellow.team: Establish a 15m exclusion zone around the hazard.
m.emergency.code.yellow.indiv: Block access to the area, keep patrons clear, await the engineering all-clear.
m.emergency.code.green.dept: Lock down the affected zone; preserve the scene for security.
m.emergency.code.green.team: Control your gate exits; log anyone leaving.
m.emergency.code.green.indiv: Note descriptions, do not pursue, report to security on your radio channel.
m.emergency.code.blue.dept: Dispatch the medical team; clear an access route.
m.emergency.code.blue.team: Open a lane from your gate to the medical post.
m.emergency.code.blue.indiv: Flag the medic in, hold a clear path, keep bystanders back.
m.emergency.code.purple.dept: Notify guest relations and the duty manager; de-escalate.
m.emergency.code.purple.team: Provide a calm buffer; route to guest services.
m.emergency.code.purple.indiv: Stay neutral and respectful, summon a lead, do not engage further.
m.emergency.code.pink.dept: Alert security and the law-enforcement liaison discreetly.
m.emergency.code.pink.team: Contain the area without alarming the crowd.
m.emergency.code.pink.indiv: Observe and report only, do not confront, mark the location on your radio channel.
m.emergency.code.indigo.dept: Activate lockdown of BOH egress; brief all leads.
m.emergency.code.indigo.team: Hold your gate; verify credentials on all exits.

m.support.faq.clock.a: Time Clock needs location access inside the venue's clock zone. Check your device's location permission, then punch again from Time Clock. Offline punches sync when you're back on network.
m.support.faq.offline.a: Screens you've visited re-serve offline with a stale banner, and punches, scans and form drafts queue until you reconnect. The Rose and your credentials stay available.
m.support.faq.rose.q: My Rose or credential won't scan.
m.support.faq.rose.a: Raise your screen brightness and try again. If the gate still rejects it, the scan journal shows the reason (expired, wrong zone, duplicate). Ask the gate lead, or reach the team below.
m.support.faq.notifications.q: I'm getting too many (or no) notifications.
m.support.faq.notifications.a: Every notification kind has its own toggle in Settings · Notification Preferences. Push needs the one-time browser permission. Re-enable it from Settings if you dismissed it.
m.support.faq.account.q: How do I export my data or delete my account?
m.support.faq.account.a: Settings · Account has both: a one-tap export of everything we hold about you, and account deletion with a 30-day grace window.

me.reviews.new.explainer.anchored: Every review is anchored to a real transaction — a booking offer, an application, a submission, a purchase order, or a project. That's what keeps ratings honest.
me.reviews.new.explainer.start: Start a review from the transaction itself: an offer in My Offers, an application in My Applications, or a submission in My Submissions. The Write a Review link there carries the transaction across so we know who and what you're rating.

me.security.twoFactor.recovery.title: Recovery Codes
me.security.twoFactor.recovery.descripti: Single-use codes for when you lose access to your authenticator. Generating new codes invalidates all existing codes.
me.security.twoFactor.recovery.hashedNot: Recovery codes are stored hashed — we can show them to you exactly once. Save them in a password manager before leaving this page.
me.security.twoFactor.confirm.regenerate: Generating new codes invalidates all existing recovery codes. Continue?
me.security.twoFactor.confirm.remove: Remove this authenticator? You'll need to re-enroll to sign in with TOTP.
me.security.twoFactor.recovery.panelBlur: Store these somewhere safe. Each code works once — for example, if you lose your authenticator. We hash codes on save, so this is the only time you'll see them.
me.security.twoFactor.title: Two-Factor Authentication
me.security.twoFactor.intro: Add a second factor — a six-digit code from an authenticator app — so a stolen password isn't enough to sign in.
me.security.twoFactor.requiredNote: Your organization requires this for your role.
me.security.twoFactor.recommendedNote: Recommended for everyone with admin access.

console.settings.capabilities.enforcemen: Make configured grants the source of truth for scanning. Preview who is affected before anything changes.
Grants are currently the source of truth. Turning this off restores the legacy blanket: everyone whose role could scan before enforcement scans everything again.
The legacy blanket is active: everyone who could scan before this system existed still scans everything. Enforcing makes the grants you configured the only source of scan access.
Nobody loses access. Everyone who scans today is covered by a role or personal grant, so this flip only removes the blanket nobody is standing on.
These people hold scan access only through the legacy blanket. The moment grants are enforced they are refused at the scanner, until a role or personal grant covers them.
These people keep scan access after the flip because a role or personal grant covers them.
Turning enforcement off re-synthesizes the legacy blanket. These people would go back to scanning everything, whatever the grant tables say.
Nobody gains anything: every scanner is already fully covered by grants.
console.settings.capabilities.title: Capabilities
console.settings.capabilities.subtitle: Add-on permissions on top of what a role already has. Grant them to a role, or to one person for a set window.
console.settings.capabilities.readOnly: You can see what this workspace grants. Changing it needs admin access.
console.settings.capabilities.enforcedBo: Grants are live. People hold what their role floor gives them, plus what is granted below, and nothing else.
console.settings.capabilities.grandfathe: Grants are not enforced yet. Everyone who could scan before still scans everything, so nothing below has bitten. Configure the grants you want, then review the flip.
console.settings.capabilities.noRoles.bo: Capabilities are granted to a role or a person. Add the roles your crew actually work as in the role catalog, then grant from here.
console.settings.capabilities.whoHoldsWh: Resolved the same way a live request resolves it: static role floor, plus crew-role grants, plus personal grants inside their window, plus the legacy blanket while enforcement is off.
console.settings.capabilities.roles.subt: The job catalog capability grants attach to. Rename freely; merge deliberately, because merging roles merges permissions.
console.settings.capabilities.roles.read: You can see the catalog. Editing it needs manager access, and merging needs admin access.
console.settings.capabilities.scanMisses: Codes the field scanned that resolved against nothing, most-seen first. A code seen forty times is a work item; a one-off is noise.
Everything the field scans is resolving. When a code misses, it lands here with a count so you can decide whether it is a catalog gap or noise.
Resolve a miss after adding the item to the catalog, or when you have judged it noise. Misses are never deleted; the queue is the record of what the field actually scanned.

legal.offerOnboarding.title: Onboarding
legal.offerOnboarding.lockedPrefix: Open your engagement letter first to unlock onboarding —
legal.offerOnboarding.criticalBody: Critical-path steps gate your credentials and first-call. Items marked ★ below.
legal.proposalSignature.consent: By signing, you consent to do business electronically and agree that your signature, name, IP address, and timestamp will be recorded as evidence of agreement, with the same legal effect as a handwritten signature.
legal.heatProposal.heroSub: A retained activation studio for the East Plaza inside Kaseya Center — concept, engineering, fabrication, install, and strike across a tiered menu of pop-up moments calibrated to partner unlocks, homestand rhythm, and signature plaza takeovers.
legal.heatProposal.heroConfidential: Confidential · Prepared for ${HEAT_DOC.contactClient} at Miami HEAT · ${HEAT_DOC.validity}
legal.heatProposal.overviewSub: Three engagement vectors, designed to compound. Each activation pulls from a tiered build menu. A monthly retainer — pick one of three tiers — holds dedicated studio capacity behind the program. Add-Ons stack onto either, on demand.
legal.heatProposal.retainerTiersSub: Three retainer tiers, scaled to your activation cadence. Each tier builds on the last — Elevated includes everything in Base, and Premium includes everything in Elevated plus exclusive lines reserved for the top tier.
legal.heatProposal.premiumExclusivesEyeb: Premium Exclusives · Reserved For The Top Tier
legal.heatProposal.premiumExclusivesTitl: Lines You Won't Find Anywhere Else.
legal.heatProposal.premiumExclusivesSub: Four lines available only at the Premium tier — not à la carte, not on Elevated, not as a one-off buy. Reserved for partners running a season-defining program with us.
legal.heatProposal.activationBuildsSub: Three build tiers, calibrated for the moment. Each tier holds a tight range from base spec to top spec, with larger architectural and retail moves quoted separately from the Add-Ons menu.
legal.heatProposal.upgradesInTier: Upgrades In This Tier · Up To ${fmtMoney(tier.ceiling)}
legal.heatProposal.tierFootnotePrefix: Larger architectural moves — chemetal cladding, container architecture, full POS retail, hospitality builds — are quoted separately from the
legal.heatProposal.tierFootnoteSuffix: and layered onto whichever tier suits the moment.
legal.heatProposal.scopeSub: Eleven scope nodes covering every fabricated and printed element plus the production services that move it from shop to plaza and back. Tier defines breadth and finish — not which nodes are touched.
legal.heatProposal.sitesSub: Three zones across the East Plaza. Each accommodates a different tier and dwell pattern; treatment scales with the moment.
legal.heatProposal.lifecycleSub: Eight phases, identical structure across every activation. The runway scales with build tier — Small condenses to five weeks, Medium runs nine, Large stretches to thirteen.
legal.heatProposal.workbackSub: Every deadline counts back from the activation date for a ${tierLabel} build. Toggle the cadence above to see how Small, Medium, and Large compare.
legal.heatProposal.investmentSub: Total program investment scales with your activation cadence and retainer tier. Pick builds off the menu, choose a retainer to hold studio capacity behind the program, and layer in the upgrades that fit the moment. Every figure covers production, fabrication, install, and strike.
legal.heatProposal.addonsCategoriesBody: Material & Finish · Functional Upgrades · Service Overlays · Logistics · Documentation. Add any number to any retainer or activation.
legal.heatProposal.investmentFootnote: Every activation runs under its own signed sign-off, layered into this master agreement. Each build tier holds a tight range from base spec to top spec, and upgrades inside that range stay inside the tier. Larger architectural moves are quoted separately from the Add-Ons menu.
legal.heatProposal.addonsSub: The full menu of optional upgrades — material, functional, service, logistics, and documentation. Pick any item, add it to any retainer or any build. Priced by request.
legal.heatProposal.depositDue: Due on written approval of each activation Statement of Work.
legal.heatProposal.balanceDue: Due five (5) business days prior to load-in. Retainer billed separately, monthly net-15.
legal.heatProposal.achWireValue: Banking detail issued with executed contract
legal.heatProposal.changeOrdersSub: Scope outside the base tier menu — issued individually or stacked into a single activation SOW.
legal.heatProposal.executeAlert: Add your signature and printed name to execute.
legal.heatProposal.authorizationSub: Signing here authorizes the baseline retainer and entitles HEAT to call activations off the tier menu under individual SOWs. Per-activation signatures live in each SOW.
legal.heatProposal.footerConfidentialBod: Prepared for ${HEAT_DOC.client}. Pricing and structural detail are confidential between the parties. © ${new Date().getFullYear()} ${HEAT_DOC.producerLong}.

legal.msaDocument.awaitingBody: Type your full legal name to formalize acceptance of this Master Services Agreement. Your typed signature, IP address, and timestamp will be captured as the audit trail.

legal.letterDocument.onboardingHint: A short list to get you ready. Try to close these out within 48 hours of acceptance so credentials can ship.
legal.letterDocument.msaOneTimeNote: One-time. Signs apply to every engagement we book you on — you won't see this step again.
legal.letterDocument.reviewGuide: Review the Salvage City Production Guide
legal.letterDocument.awaitingBody: Type your full legal name below to formalize acceptance. Your typed signature, IP address, and timestamp will be captured as the audit trail.
legal.letterDocument.footerMsaOnFile: This engagement is subject to your Independent Contractor Master Services Agreement signed ${formatDate(activeMsa.signed_at, "long")} · v${activeMsa.version}.

legal.letterDocument.footerMsaSeparateEm: This engagement is subject to our Independent Contractor Master Services Agreement — your personal copy will arrive in a separate email and is required before your first service day.

marketing.glossaryContent.advancing.shor: The pre-event workflow of finalizing every operational detail with each stakeholder.
marketing.glossaryContent.advancing.long: Advancing is the production process of confirming every operational detail with each stakeholder (artists, vendors, venues, crew) before load-in. It covers technical riders, hospitality riders, stage plots, input lists, ground transport, hotel blocks, insurance certificates, credentials, and any per-stop variances. Strong advancing reduces show-day surprise; weak advancing creates day-of fires that cost real money. In modern stacks, advancing is tracked as typed deliverables with status, owner, due date, and history, not as PDF chains in email.
marketing.glossaryContent.kbyg.short: A pre-event briefing document scoped to a stakeholder's role.
marketing.glossaryContent.kbyg.long: KBYG, Know Before You Go, is a stakeholder-facing document that gathers everything someone needs to show up to an event prepared: schedule, set times, credentials, parking, dressing-room location, comms channels, PPE requirements, evacuation plan, code of conduct. The strongest KBYG implementations scope by role: a guest sees parking and schedule, a crew member sees radio channels and call sheet, an artist sees rider and hospitality. Same canonical content; different views.
marketing.glossaryContent.ros.short: The minute-by-minute schedule of an event: cues, transitions, departments.
marketing.glossaryContent.ros.long: Run-of-Show, or ROS, is the canonical minute-by-minute schedule of an event from doors through strike. Each row carries a cue number, a time, a duration, a department (audio, lighting, video, scenic, security, hospitality), and a note. Show callers and production managers run from the ROS in real time. The ROS publishes to portals so artists see set times, crew see calls, and vendors see delivery windows, all from one canonical record. A weak ROS lives in a shared document that nobody trusts after the second revision.
marketing.glossaryContent.load-in.short: The process of moving gear and crew into a venue and building the show.
marketing.glossaryContent.load-in.long: Load-in is the period (from a few hours to several days) during which trucks unload, crew rigs the show, audio and lighting strike configurations, scenic elements assemble, and the event takes physical form in the venue. Load-in windows are negotiated with the venue and constrain everything downstream: rigging access times, freight elevator slots, parking, security clearances. Production managers track load-in against a per-department schedule and against the venue's hard constraints.
marketing.glossaryContent.load-out.short: The process of breaking down the show and clearing the venue.
marketing.glossaryContent.load-out.long: Load-out is the reverse of load-in: the show ends, doors clear, and crew dismantles audio, lighting, scenic, and rigging in reverse-build order to load trucks. Venues impose hard exit deadlines (often tied to next-day load-ins for the next event), and load-out velocity often determines whether overtime hours kick in. Tight load-out planning saves money and labor.
marketing.glossaryContent.strike.short: The full breakdown and removal of all event production from a venue.
marketing.glossaryContent.strike.long: Strike is the final phase of an event: every piece of gear, scenic element, signage, cable run, and consumable is removed and the venue returned to its pre-event state. Strike is typically the highest-injury period of a show: crew is tired, deadlines are tight, and heavy gear is moving fast. That makes safety briefings, PPE compliance, and load-out scheduling especially important.
marketing.glossaryContent.call-sheet.sho: A per-day document telling each crew member when and where to report.
marketing.glossaryContent.call-sheet.lon: A call sheet is the daily roster published to every crew member: their call time (when to arrive), department, role, location, and any specific instructions. Originally a film-production artifact, call sheets are now standard in events and touring. Modern call sheets publish to a stakeholder portal or mobile app rather than as PDF attachments, with automatic conflict detection against double-booked crew.
marketing.glossaryContent.show-call.shor: The act of calling cues during the live event.
marketing.glossaryContent.show-call.long: Show calling is the live act of calling cues (lighting cues, audio cues, video cues, automation cues, talent intros, security pivots) during the show. The show caller works from the ROS, communicating over comms to each department. The show caller's role is the single most important live-event coordination position. A clean show call is invisible; a broken show call is the difference between a great show and a recoverable one.
marketing.glossaryContent.set-time.short: The scheduled start time of an artist's performance.
marketing.glossaryContent.set-time.long: Set time is the moment an artist takes the stage. Set times are negotiated between booking and production, locked into the ROS, and published to artists via their advancing portal. Festivals typically have hard set time windows with strict changeover periods; concerts have softer windows tied to support-act performance. Conflicts between set times (overlapping stages with shared crew) surface in modern schedule tools automatically.
marketing.glossaryContent.doors.short: The time the venue opens to the public.
marketing.glossaryContent.doors.long: Doors is the moment the venue's public-facing entry opens. Everything inside the venue must be show-ready by doors: sound check complete, security in position, hospitality stocked, gates and scanners online. Doors is one of the show's hard gates: nothing slips past it. ROS counts forward from doors to set times and backward from doors to load-in milestones.
marketing.glossaryContent.technical-ride: An artist's production-spec document covering audio, lighting, video, stage, and power.

A technical rider is the document the artist's team sends ahead of a performance specifying the exact production setup required: audio I/O, monitor configuration, lighting needs, video playback, stage dimensions, riser locations, power requirements. Riders range from a single page (small touring acts) to dozens of pages (headliner festivals). Modern advancing systems track riders as typed deliverables, versioned, status-tracked, and acknowledged in writing.
marketing.glossaryContent.hospitality-ri: An artist's non-production needs: food, drink, dressing room, transport.

A hospitality rider lists the non-production needs of the artist's traveling party: catering preferences, dressing room stocking (drinks, snacks, towels), ground transport requirements, hotel preferences, runner allowances. Hospitality riders include both contractual obligations (catering by X time) and preferences (specific brands, dietary restrictions). Production teams treat hospitality riders as binding even when they're informal.
marketing.glossaryContent.stage-plot.sho: A diagram showing the placement of equipment, talent, and monitors onstage.
marketing.glossaryContent.stage-plot.lon: A stage plot is the bird's-eye-view diagram of an artist's onstage configuration: where drums sit, where monitors face, where mic stands rise, where amps anchor. Stage plots inform the audio engineer, the monitor engineer, the lighting designer, and the staging team. They're submitted as PDF attachments to advancing and are increasingly stored as typed artifacts with revision history.
marketing.glossaryContent.input-list.sho: An audio-channel-by-channel map of microphones, DI boxes, and patch points.
marketing.glossaryContent.input-list.lon: An input list is the audio team's reference: every channel on the console, what's plugged into it (mic model, DI box, line in), and how it's labeled. Input lists are bundled with the technical rider, given to the front-of-house and monitor engineers in advance, and used to pre-patch the console before sound check. A clean input list saves hours at load-in.
marketing.glossaryContent.coi.short: A vendor's proof of insurance coverage for general liability and related risks.
marketing.glossaryContent.coi.long: A Certificate of Insurance, or COI, is the document a vendor submits to prove they carry general liability, auto liability, workers' compensation, and umbrella coverage at the limits a venue or producer requires. COIs name the producer or venue as additional insured and list policy effective and expiration dates. Production teams gate purchase orders behind a current COI: if the cert is expired, the PO blocks.
marketing.glossaryContent.w9.short: A US tax form vendors complete to enable 1099 reporting.
marketing.glossaryContent.w9.long: Form W-9 is the IRS document a US vendor completes to give the producer their taxpayer ID number and business classification, enabling year-end 1099-NEC issuance for non-employee compensation. Production teams collect W-9s during vendor onboarding and gate payouts on having a current W-9 on file. Foreign vendors complete W-8 series forms instead.
marketing.glossaryContent.credentials.sh: Physical or digital passes granting access to event-restricted areas.
marketing.glossaryContent.credentials.lo: Credentials, also called passes, are the physical (laminate, wristband) or digital (QR, NFC) tokens that grant access to event-restricted areas. Tiered credentials (AAA all-access, working pass, talent pass, crew pass, vendor pass, guest pass) carry different access levels: front-of-house, backstage, dressing rooms, production offices, rigging zones. Credentials are issued from a manifest, scanned at zone gates, and revoked when someone leaves their role.
marketing.glossaryContent.all-access.sho: The highest credential tier, granting access to every zone.
marketing.glossaryContent.all-access.lon: All-Access, often abbreviated AAA, is the top credential tier issued at a production: it grants access to every zone, including talent dressing rooms, production offices, and rigging areas. AAA credentials are tightly controlled; the AAA list is small and runs through senior production leadership. The credentials manager treats AAA as the most audit-sensitive credential tier.
marketing.glossaryContent.working-pass.s: A credential identifying crew or vendors actively working the event.
marketing.glossaryContent.working-pass.l: A working pass is the credential that identifies a person as crew or vendor actively working an event. Working passes typically grant access to backstage, production offices, and any zone needed for their role, but exclude high-restriction areas like artist dressing rooms unless the role requires it. Working pass holders are vetted at credential pickup and may sign acknowledgements at check-in.
marketing.glossaryContent.manifest.short: The canonical roster of who is credentialed, ticketed, or accommodated.
marketing.glossaryContent.manifest.long: A manifest is the canonical list: of credentialed crew, of ticketed guests, of accommodation-block residents, of vehicle passengers. Manifests reconcile against the gate scanner: every credential or ticket on the manifest scans to accepted; everything else scans to denied. A clean manifest is the difference between a smooth gate and a 30-minute line.
marketing.glossaryContent.gate-scan.shor: The act of scanning a credential or ticket to verify entry rights.
marketing.glossaryContent.gate-scan.long: Gate scan is the single most time-sensitive operation at a live event: a credential or ticket presents at the gate, scans against the manifest, and resolves to accepted, denied, voided, or used in under a hundred milliseconds. Modern gate scanners run as offline-first mobile apps so they keep working when venue cellular drops; scans queue locally and replay in order when signal returns. Race conditions (the same ticket presenting at two gates) resolve atomically: every ticket scans exactly once.
marketing.glossaryContent.last-entry.sho: The deadline after which the gate stops admitting new attendees.
marketing.glossaryContent.last-entry.lon: Last entry is the time after which the gate stops admitting guests. Most events publish a last-entry time in the KBYG so attendees know to arrive before it. Late entries are sometimes accommodated for VIP or talent guests via separate channels but rarely for the general public. The gate scanner enforces last entry: scans after the cutoff resolve to denied.
marketing.glossaryContent.rigging.short: The art of suspending lighting, audio, video, and scenic from venue structure.
marketing.glossaryContent.rigging.long: Rigging is the discipline of suspending heavy production gear (lighting trusses, line arrays, video walls, scenic) safely from a venue's structural points. Rigging crews work with the venue's rig plot to identify load-bearing points, calculate point loads and distributed loads, and rig motors, chains, and trusses. Rigging is among the highest-injury disciplines on a production; reputable shops use third-party rigging inspections before doors.
marketing.glossaryContent.point-load.sho: The weight applied at a single suspension point on a venue's rig structure.
marketing.glossaryContent.point-load.lon: A point load is the static weight applied at a single suspension point in a venue's rigging structure: a single beam clamp, a single motor pickup. Venues publish rated capacities per point (e.g., 1,000 lbs per beam), and riggers must ensure no single point exceeds rated capacity. Point load math is on every rigging plot; modern production tools track it per suspension point with margins.
marketing.glossaryContent.dynamic-load.s: The peak load applied during motion or sudden change; it exceeds the static load.
marketing.glossaryContent.dynamic-load.l: Dynamic load is the peak force applied to a rigging point during motion (a lift, a release, a sudden stop) and can substantially exceed the static load. Rigging engineers calculate dynamic loads with safety factors (typically 7:1 over peak dynamic) to ensure structural integrity. The dynamic load math is why a motor with a static rating of 1,000 lbs isn't lifting 1,000 lbs of gear; it's lifting some fraction with the dynamic factor preserved.
marketing.glossaryContent.foh.short: The audience-facing side of the venue, where the mixing console lives.
marketing.glossaryContent.foh.long: Front of House, FOH, refers to the audience-facing side of a venue and the audio mixing position located there. The FOH engineer mixes what the audience hears through the main sound system. FOH is also where the lighting console often sits, giving the lighting designer line-of-sight to the stage. In broader production usage, FOH refers to anything visible to the audience (signage, ushers, ticket scanners).
marketing.glossaryContent.boh.short: The non-public side of the venue: dressing rooms, production offices, loading.
marketing.glossaryContent.boh.long: Back of House, BOH, covers everything not visible to the audience: dressing rooms, production offices, loading docks, green rooms, catering, freight elevators. BOH access is credentialed: only working passes, talent passes, and higher-tier credentials get in. The line between FOH and BOH is the operational divide of a venue.
marketing.glossaryContent.comms.short: The wired or wireless intercom system connecting departments during a show.
marketing.glossaryContent.comms.long: Comms, short for communications, is the intercom infrastructure that lets show callers, stage managers, lighting, audio, video, and security talk to each other in real time during load-in, show call, and load-out. Comms channels separate by department; the show caller typically sits on a master channel that punches into each department's channel as needed. Wired comms (Clear-Com) and wireless comms (Riedel Bolero) coexist on most large productions.
marketing.glossaryContent.rfi.short: A formal question raised during production with a routed, official answer.
marketing.glossaryContent.rfi.long: A Request for Information, RFI, is a formal, tracked question raised during production: a contractor asks a producer for clarification on scope, a vendor asks for a spec confirmation, a producer asks a venue for an access detail. Each RFI has a recipient (ball-in-court), a clock (due date), and an official answer that becomes part of the project record. Construction projects pioneered the RFI pattern; live-event production borrows it for fabrication, scenic, and major build-outs.
marketing.glossaryContent.submittal.shor: A formal product or spec submission for approval before installation.
marketing.glossaryContent.submittal.long: A submittal is the formal proposal a contractor or vendor sends for review and approval before installing or fabricating: a paint color, a lighting fixture model, a custom-built piece, a finish material. Submittals route through a reviewer, get approved-as-noted, rejected, or approved-with-revisions, and become part of the project record. Strong submittal workflows prevent costly mid-build pivots.
marketing.glossaryContent.punch-list.sho: The list of remaining items to fix before a build is considered complete.
marketing.glossaryContent.punch-list.lon: A punch list, also called a snag list, is the running register of items that need attention before a build or installation is considered complete: paint touch-ups, wire dressings, finish corrections, missing labels, alignment issues. Each item has a location, a responsible trade, a photo, and a due date. The punch list closes via a final walk-through where each item is signed off.
marketing.glossaryContent.change-order.s: A formal modification to scope, schedule, or price after the contract is signed.
marketing.glossaryContent.change-order.l: A change order is the formal mechanism for modifying a signed contract: scope changes, schedule extensions, price adjustments. Change orders quantify the work in question, price it, and require approval before execution. In events and fabrication, change orders prevent the all-too-common 'the producer asked for this, we did it, now no one wants to pay for it' pattern. Every change order writes to the project budget on approval.
marketing.glossaryContent.show-ready.sho: A gated status indicating a project is complete and cleared for doors.
marketing.glossaryContent.show-ready.lon: Show-ready is the gate that has to clear before doors open: every inspection signed, every punch item resolved, every credential printed, every comms checked. Modern production tools enforce show-ready as a hard gate: invoicing, doors, or final payment can't proceed without it. Show-ready is the producer's equivalent of construction's substantial-completion.
marketing.glossaryContent.incident-repor: A documented record of a safety, security, or medical event during a production.

An incident report is the documented record of any safety, security, or medical event during a production: an injury, a near-miss, a security intervention, a medical response, an environmental release. Incident reports capture the time, location, witnesses, photos, and resolution. OSHA-recordable incidents flow into the annual 300 log. Anonymous reporting channels, where the reporter chooses what they disclose, are increasingly standard for safeguarding and harassment reporting.
marketing.glossaryContent.osha-300.short: The US workplace-injury and illness log required by federal OSHA regulations.
marketing.glossaryContent.osha-300.long: The OSHA Form 300 is the log of work-related injuries and illnesses that US employers with more than ten employees maintain annually. Each entry records the case, the employee, the date, the diagnosis, and the outcome (days away, restricted work, transfer). The 300A annual summary is posted publicly Feb-Apr each year. Production teams with field-injury exposure run this against the 300 and 300A as part of their year-end compliance cadence.
marketing.glossaryContent.near-miss.shor: An incident with potential for harm that didn't result in injury or damage.
marketing.glossaryContent.near-miss.long: A near-miss is an event with potential for harm (a falling object that didn't hit anyone, an unsecured cable that almost tripped a crew member, an electrical issue caught before contact) that didn't result in injury or property damage. Robust safety cultures capture near-misses at higher rates than recordable incidents because they're the leading indicator. Each near-miss is an opportunity to fix a structural risk before it costs someone.
marketing.glossaryContent.per-diem.short: A daily allowance paid to crew or talent for meals and incidentals while traveling.
marketing.glossaryContent.per-diem.long: Per diem is the daily cash allowance paid to crew or talent for meals and incidentals while traveling for a production. Per diem rates vary by city and country (GSA publishes US federal rates) and may differ for crew vs. union-rate crew vs. talent. Per diem typically settles in cash on a per-show basis or accumulates on a paystub. Tour managers track per diem against the city × day rate matrix.
marketing.glossaryContent.advance.short: A cash payment made ahead of a production to cover anticipated expenses.
marketing.glossaryContent.advance.long: A cash advance, in production-finance context, is money paid ahead of a show to cover anticipated expenses: per diem, runner cash, vendor deposits, on-the-fly purchases. Cash advances reconcile against receipts and remaining cash on settlement night. Note: 'advancing' (the pre-event workflow) and 'cash advance' (the finance instrument) are different concepts that share a name; context distinguishes them.
marketing.glossaryContent.settlement.sho: The post-show financial reconciliation between artist, promoter, and venue.
marketing.glossaryContent.settlement.lon: Settlement is the post-show financial close-out: reconciling box-office numbers, deducting venue costs, paying artist guarantees and back-end percentages, settling per diems, and producing a settlement sheet signed by the artist's representative. Settlements happen at the venue, often the same night as the show, and depend on accurate, real-time box-office data. Mature production stacks run settlement off live data, not next-day spreadsheets.
marketing.glossaryContent.xpms.short: A shared standard for experiential production work: phases, departments, disciplines, and canonical work atoms.
marketing.glossaryContent.xpms.long: XPMS is the Experiential Production Management Standard, a structured taxonomy that gives live-event and experiential production a common language for planning, executing, and auditing work. Version 2.5, the current release, defines 9 gated phases, 10 department classes, 79 disciplines, and 406 canonical work atoms, each atom carrying 44 fields in the standard's SSOT Bible. Because tasks, budget lines, catalogs, and compliance checks all anchor to the same taxonomy, teams and tools interoperate without renegotiating vocabulary on every project. The ATLVS ecosystem ships XPMS 2.5 as its default base kit: a new organization arrives pre-seeded with the standard's phases, department classes, and atom catalog, and layers its own customizations on top.
marketing.glossaryContent.gated-phases.s: The nine sequential XPMS production phases, each closed by a gate: Discover, Design, Advance, Procure, Build, Install, Operate, Amplify, Close.
marketing.glossaryContent.gated-phases.l: Gated phases are the sequential macro arc of a production under XPMS: Discover, Design, Advance, Procure, Build, Install, Operate, Amplify, Close. Each phase ends at a gate, a checkpoint where defined criteria must clear before downstream work proceeds, which keeps procurement from outrunning design and settlement from starting before operations close. Amplify is the XPMS 2.5 addition at gate 8: content capture, media and press, broadcast, and post-event campaigns treated as first-class production work with their own gate. In the ATLVS ecosystem every project carries its XPMS phase, and the console renders the lifecycle as a live checklist from sell through settle.
marketing.glossaryContent.coordinate-mat: The XPMS lens that crosses 9 phases with 10 department classes, giving every task one of 90 coordinates.

The coordinate matrix is the XPMS planning lens formed by crossing the standard's 9 gated phases with its 10 department classes, yielding 90 coordinates. Any unit of production work lands at exactly one coordinate (Technology work during Install, Executive work during Discover), which makes gaps and overloads visible at a glance: an empty coordinate is unplanned scope, an overloaded one is a staffing risk. The ATLVS ecosystem uses the coordinate matrix as a task and forecasting lens, rolling tasks and hours up by phase and department class so producers can see where the work actually sits.
marketing.glossaryContent.urid.short: The XPMS identifier grammar DEPT.DISCIPLINE.CATEGORY: a stable, sortable address for every kind of production work.
marketing.glossaryContent.urid.long: A URID is an identifier following the XPMS grammar DEPT.DISCIPLINE.CATEGORY: the department class, the discipline within it, and the category of work. The grammar gives every kind of production work a stable, sortable address, so a budget line, a task in a plan, and an item in a catalog can all point to the same identifier and reconcile cleanly. XPMS 2.5 spans 10 department classes and 79 disciplines under this grammar. In the ATLVS ecosystem, URIDs key the catalog and knowledge surfaces, tying priced atoms and templates back to the standard.
marketing.glossaryContent.work-atom.shor: The canonical unit of production work in XPMS, fully specified so it can be scheduled, priced, and audited consistently.
marketing.glossaryContent.work-atom.long: A work atom is the smallest canonical unit of production work defined by XPMS: one discrete, repeatable piece of work with a stable identity and a full specification. Each atom carries 44 fields in the standard's SSOT Bible, covering identity, phase and department coordinates, dependencies, and crew and compliance requirements, so the same atom can be scheduled, priced, and audited the same way on every project. XPMS 2.5 defines 406 canonical atoms. The ATLVS ecosystem seeds new organizations with the atom catalog as their starter task and asset vocabulary, and ties tasks and budget lines back to atoms.
marketing.glossaryContent.department-cla: One of the 10 top-level XPMS divisions of production work, numbered 0000 Executive through 9000 Technology.

Department classes are the 10 top-level divisions of production work in XPMS, numbered in thousands from 0000 Executive through 9000 Technology. Every discipline, work atom, and budget line rolls up to exactly one class, which lets a phase-by-department coordinate matrix, a GL code structure, and an org chart all share the same spine. The numbering doubles as a cost-center scheme, so finance and operations sort the same way without a mapping table. The ATLVS ecosystem seeds each organization's cost centers from the 10 XPMS department classes and maps position libraries and forecasting lenses onto them.
marketing.glossaryContent.advance-packet: A scoped information packet sent to one counterparty during advancing, with a structured return expected against a deadline.

An advance packet is the unit of exchange in a production advance: a scoped bundle of information going out to one counterparty (an artist team, a vendor, a delegation) and a structured submission expected back by a deadline. A packet carries only the sections that counterparty needs, such as technical requirements, travel, credentials, or safety, and the return comes back as structured rows the production can aggregate. In the ATLVS ecosystem, advance packets are composed per project, sent per recipient with a tracked delivery funnel from queued through submitted, and chased automatically as deadlines approach.
marketing.glossaryContent.daily-log.shor: The per-day record of what happened on site: crew counts, work performed, deliveries, weather, and incidents.
marketing.glossaryContent.daily-log.long: A daily log is the running per-day record of a build or event site: headcount by trade, work performed, deliveries received, weather, delays, visitors, and any incidents. Construction sites have kept daily logs for decades because they settle disputes; when a schedule or payment argument surfaces months later, the log is the contemporaneous evidence. Live-event builds borrow the practice for load-in, show days, and strike. In the ATLVS ecosystem, daily logs are filed from the field on mobile and roll up to the project record alongside tasks and incident reports.
marketing.glossaryContent.shift-handover: The structured transfer of open items, risks, and site state from an outgoing shift to the incoming one.

A shift handover is the controlled moment when one shift passes responsibility to the next: open items, in-progress work, active risks, radio channels, and anything the incoming lead must know before the outgoing lead walks. Around-the-clock operations (festival builds, 24-hour load-ins, security posts) live and die on handover quality, because most cross-shift failures trace to something the last shift knew and never wrote down. Strong handovers are structured and logged. In the ATLVS ecosystem, shift handovers are captured as structured field records tied to the shift, visible to the incoming crew before they clock in.
marketing.glossaryContent.chain-of-custo: The documented, unbroken trail of who held an asset from checkout to return.

Chain of custody is the documented trail of possession for an asset: who checked it out, when it moved, who signed at each transfer, and when it came back. The practice comes from evidence handling and applies wherever gear is valuable, regulated, or shared: radios, scanners, vehicles, tools, medical kits. A broken chain is how a two-thousand-dollar radio disappears with nobody accountable. In the ATLVS ecosystem, custody events are logged per asset as scans and signatures, so every transfer writes a journal row and the current holder is answerable in one query.
marketing.glossaryContent.site-ops.short: The day-to-day operation of an event site: gates, power, water, waste, traffic, and everything that keeps the ground working.
marketing.glossaryContent.site-ops.long: Site ops, short for site operations, is the discipline of running the physical event site day to day: gates and perimeter, power and generators, water and waste, traffic and parking, weather monitoring, radios, and the dozens of small systems a site depends on. Site ops starts before load-in and ends after strike, and on multi-day events it runs around the clock. Where production builds the show, site ops keeps the ground under it working. In the ATLVS ecosystem, site ops runs through the COMPVSS field app: operational ledgers, shift coverage, and incident capture from anywhere on site.
marketing.glossaryContent.deskless-workf: Workers who do their jobs away from a desk: stagehands, riggers, security, medics, drivers, and stewards.

The deskless workforce is the large majority of the global workforce that does not work at a desk; in live events, that means stagehands, riggers, security, medics, drivers, caterers, and stewards. Deskless workers rarely carry a company laptop or live in email, so tooling built for office workers fails them in the field; what works is mobile-first, offline-tolerant software with schedules, time clock, tasks, and comms in one place. The COMPVSS field app in the ATLVS ecosystem is built for exactly this population: an offline-first PWA where crew clock in, see shifts, file incidents, and read their event guide from a phone.
marketing.glossaryContent.white-label.sh: Presenting stakeholder-facing surfaces under the organization's or event's own brand rather than the software vendor's.
marketing.glossaryContent.white-label.lo: White-label is the practice of shipping stakeholder-facing surfaces (portals, documents, guides, booking pages) under the producing organization's or the event's own brand. Artists, vendors, and clients see the promoter's name and design, which reads as one professional operation and keeps trust where the relationship lives. White-labeling matters most on artifacts that leave the building: proposals, settlement sheets, advance packets, and guest guides. In the ATLVS ecosystem, documents and reports carry brand modes for org and client white-labeling, and portal surfaces render under event branding.
Live Events
Venue residencies, club nights, one-offs
