Only what you share. Touring and promoter reps can be invited into a single event with scoped access. They never see the rest of your calendar or another promoter's event.
How does shift handover work?
The outgoing lead logs a handover note against the shift, with open punch items attached. The incoming crew opens the app and sees exactly what is unresolved. No whiteboard required.
marketing.industriesContent.trade-shows-: Trade Shows & Exhibitions
Exhibit halls, booth builds, show-floor operations

Move-in to move-out on one field app. COMPVSS schedules labor calls per hall, runs booth punch lists to zero before show open, scans crew at the dock, and tracks every cart and lift checked out on the floor.
Trade Shows & Exhibitions
Move-In to Move-Out, On the Floor.

An exhibit hall build is dozens of crews from different companies working the same floor against the same deadline. COMPVSS gives every crew its call time, its punch list, and its dock access from a phone, and gives you the whole floor in one view.

We are a general contractor with subs from six companies. Can they all work in one system?
Yes. Each subcontractor joins the project with scoped access. They see their own calls, punches, and dock windows. You see the whole floor rolled up by hall and by company.
Does the app work in the middle of a convention hall?
Yes. COMPVSS is offline-first. Punches, scans, and time punches queue on the device and sync when signal returns, which matters when ten thousand exhibitors are sharing one access point.
Can we run union labor calls through the platform?
Yes. Calls are scheduled per crew with call times, positions, and confirmations. Time punches are geo-verified and export for payroll, and the daily log preserves who worked which hall.
Conferences
Summits, multi-track programs, general sessions

For the production teams behind multi-track conferences: room turnovers on punch lists, AV crews scheduled per session block, check-in scanning at registration and session doors, and briefings that reach every crew phone before the first keynote.
Conferences
Forty Sessions a Day. Every Room Turn Logged.

A conference is a production that resets itself every ninety minutes. COMPVSS runs the turn schedule, the room-set punch lists, and the door scans from the phones your crew already carries, so the 2 p.m. breakout looks as sharp as the opening keynote.
room punch closed

Crew Scheduling
AV, rigging, and floor crews scheduled per session block. Changes reach the right phones immediately.
Check-In Scan
Registration and session-door scanning on any phone, with offline queue for crowded ballroom corridors.
Briefings & Guides
Per-persona day-of guides: crew sees run notes and escalations, staff sees the attendee-facing script.
How is this different from your Corporate Events coverage?

Corporate Events covers the broader client-side program: proposals, stakeholder portals, compliance. Conferences is the production floor itself: session turns, AV crews, door scans, briefings. Many teams use both angles on the same project.
Can we handle a campus spread across three hotels?
Yes. Locations and rooms are modeled per venue, crews are scheduled per building, and the schedule view shows travel gaps between properties so a crew is never booked in two hotels at once.
What happens when the program changes at 11 a.m.?
Update the session block once and the change pushes to every affected crew phone. The old time disappears from their schedule, the new one appears, and the turn punch list moves with it.

Weddings & Private Events
The Client Sees the Room. You See the Punch List.

A high-end private build is a week of load-ins on a site with no loading dock and a client walk-through that cannot slip. COMPVSS runs the build schedule, the vendor arrivals, and the final punch from your crew's phones, even where the estate has no signal.
punch item filed

Our clients expect discretion. Who can see event data?
Only the people you invite, at the access level you give them. Each event is walled off, external vendors see only their own scope, and the audit log records every view and change.
Does this work at a remote estate with no cell coverage?
Yes. COMPVSS is offline-first. Check-ins, punches, and time punches queue on the device and sync when crews get back into coverage. The build does not pause for the signal.
We are a twelve-person company. Is this built for teams our size?
Yes. Most private-event production companies run lean and hire in per build. The core team lives in the app, and per-event crew and vendors join with scoped access for just that project.

Government & Municipal
A Public Event Is a Public Record.

A street festival answers to more agencies than any private show: permits, public safety, after-action review. COMPVSS documents the operation as it happens, from volunteer check-in to the last incident report, so the record writes itself.

Can volunteers who are not city employees use the app?
Yes. Volunteers join the event with scoped access: their shift, their zone, their check-in, and the emergency guide. They never touch staff scheduling or internal records.
Will the records satisfy an after-action review or a records request?

Can we pilot this on a single event before a wider procurement?

Yes. Most agencies start with one festival or one season. A single-event pilot exercises the full system, and the data carries forward if you expand. Contact us about procurement paperwork; we have done it before.

Half our crew is new every semester. How fast can they start?

A student joins from an invite, sees their shifts, and reads the role guide on their phone. Most crews are working their first event the same week. The guide carries the training load a veteran used to.
Can we run many small events at once across campus?

marketing.teams.tour-managers.blurb: The single accountable owner of an entire touring run: advancing, finance, logistics, crew, settlement.
marketing.teams.tour-managers.hero.title: Tour Management, On Modern Rails.
marketing.teams.tour-managers.hero.body: The TM stack hasn't changed in twenty years. A desktop app and a binder. ATLVS retunes the same primitives for cloud-native, real portals, offline mobile, Aurora AI assistance, per-org pricing.
Sixteen typed deliverables per stop. Riders, hospitality, stage plots, hotel blocks, ground transport. Portals for venues, vendors, drivers.
Crew + talent see their day sheet in the portal. No more PDF email chains. Updates publish; nobody asks 'is this the latest?'
City × day × rate → finance. Pay-day reconciliation runs from the same record. Per-diems flow into payroll automatically.
Box office, venue cost, guarantees, back-end. Settled at the venue with a signed sheet, off live data.
Vehicle runs with driver, vehicle, manifest, POD. Drivers see their runs in a portal with no app to install.
Weather hold, security incident, evacuation: pre-approved templates publish in one tap from the road.
marketing.teams.tour-managers.pain-point: Day sheets emailed as PDFs and immediately out of date
Per-diem math redone every Monday morning
Settlement spreadsheet built from scratch every show
Driver runs tracked in a chat thread until they're not
marketing.teams.tour-managers.faqs.0.q: Will this replace our desktop touring stack?
marketing.teams.tour-managers.faqs.0.a: Yes, for modern touring orgs. We carry advancing, day sheets, and per-stop variance, and add cloud-native multi-user, real portals, offline mobile, Aurora AI, and per-org pricing. The old tools win on muscle memory; we win on every spec.
marketing.teams.tour-managers.faqs.1.q: Can we run multiple tours simultaneously?
marketing.teams.tour-managers.faqs.1.a: Yes. The 21-day look-ahead crosses projects so you see what's load-in this week and which crew is available next.
marketing.teams.tour-managers.faqs.2.a: Box-office data lands in finance throughout the show; settlement night you reconcile against guarantees, deductions, and back-end. A signed PDF exports for the venue/promoter.
marketing.teams.production-managers.blur: The build owner: RFIs, submittals, daily logs, punch, inspections, show-ready.
marketing.teams.production-managers.hero: Run The Build Like A Build. Just Faster.
The PM stack borrows from construction (ball-in-court RFIs, submittals, daily logs, punch lists, change orders) at show-day velocity, built for the production calendar.
marketing.teams.production-managers.work: Question, recipient, official answer, due date. Open RFIs surface on every project dashboard. Closes that take 14 days on construction close in 24 hours here.
Weather pulled from forecast, manpower from time entries, photos from the gallery. One record per day. Recap writes itself.
Item, location, trade, photo, due date. Show-ready gate enforces it. Doors don't open until punch closes.
Ten built-in templates (rigging, electrical, fire/life-safety, structural, ADA, food service, FOH, BOH, dressing rooms, broadcast). PDF output the AHJ accepts.
Quantified, priced, approved before the work happens. Writes to budget on accept.
Vendor portal with COIs, W-9s, POs, payouts via Stripe Connect. Compliance gates POs automatically.
marketing.teams.production-managers.pain: Daily logs in a notebook no one reads after wrap
Punch list scattered across phone notes apps
AHJ asks for inspection evidence the morning of show
marketing.teams.production-managers.faqs: How does this fit production timelines?

Same primitives at production velocity. Construction tooling is built for 18-month projects with deep BIM. Event production runs in 4-12 week cycles; we drop the heavy procurement-bidding and tighten the loop on what matters.
Can we run multiple builds in flight?
Yes. Production tier is unlimited users and unlimited projects. The 21-day look-ahead crosses projects.
marketing.teams.stage-managers.blurb: The show caller: ROS, comms, cue books, real-time direction.
marketing.teams.stage-managers.hero.body: The SM lives on the ROS. ATLVS gives you cue numbers, departments, departmental notes: the cue book shape, with everything else on the production hanging off it.
marketing.teams.stage-managers.workflows: Cue number, time, duration, department, notes. Publishes to portals so artist sees set time, crew sees call, vendor sees delivery window.
Reads like a cue book. Filterable per department. Comms channels per cue when relevant.
Call sheet flows from the schedule to the portal to the ICS. Per-crew call times based on ROS dependency.
Double-booked crew, gear, or venues surface before they cost you.
Show-call notes captured live; post-show debrief writes itself from the timestamped log.
Artists see their set time in the portal. Changes notify automatically; nobody texts asking 'is doors still 8?'
marketing.teams.stage-managers.pain-poin: ROS in a shared Google Doc that two people edit simultaneously
Crew call texted out the morning of and never reconciled to the budget
Show notes captured in a notebook that lives in someone's bag
marketing.teams.stage-managers.faqs.0.q: Does it replace our cue-call software?
marketing.teams.stage-managers.faqs.0.a: For the call book itself, usually yes. For comms intercom, no: we don't replace dedicated intercom hardware. We're the canonical schedule and notes layer that lives above the comms hardware.
marketing.teams.festival-directors.blurb: Multi-day, multi-stage, 15k+ guest operations from one console.
Run The Festival. Not The Spreadsheet.
Festivals run on six concurrent operations: ticketing + gate, advancing + ROS, vendor + procurement, safety + medical, F&B + hospitality, marketing + sponsorship. ATLVS is one platform for all six.
marketing.teams.festival-directors.workf: Sub-100ms scan offline-queued. Multiple gates concurrent. Zero duplicates under load.
Per-stage cue book with changeover windows. Artist set times, hospitality runs, sound checks.
Guest, crew, artist, vendor, sponsor: each sees their version. One source, scoped renders.
COIs, W-9s, payouts via Stripe Connect. POs gate behind a current COI. Audit-ready.
Sponsor portal with activation specs, asset library, entitlements, reporting on delivery.
Photos, P&L, recordable incidents, gate throughput. One PDF on settlement night.
marketing.teams.festival-directors.pain-: Ticketing on one platform, gate on another, no reconciliation
Vendor COIs in a folder that's six months out of date
Sponsor reporting put together by hand the week after wrap
How many guests can the platform handle?
Tested at 15,000-guest single gates with concurrent scanning, sub-100ms, offline-queued. The bottleneck is your network, not us.
Can sponsors get a branded portal?
Yes. Per-project branded portal scoped by RLS. Each sponsor sees only their activation, assets, and reporting.
marketing.teams.site-managers.blurb: Build site ownership: load-in, inspections, daily logs, strike.
marketing.teams.site-managers.hero.title: The Build Site, Documented.
marketing.teams.site-managers.hero.body: Site managers own the build site from load-in through strike. ATLVS captures the day (weather, manpower, inspections, photos, punch, change orders) and turns it into the post-show wrap automatically.
Weather, manpower, equipment on site, work performed, photos. Auto-populates from time entries.
Walk inspection on the phone. Pass/fail per item, photo evidence, sign-off. PDF output for the AHJ.
Per-project gallery, EXIF preserved, geo-tagged. Daily logs auto-bundle the day's photos.
Item, location, photo, due date. Show-ready gate enforces resolution before doors.
Reverse-build order, hard exit deadline, OT triggers tracked.
Field-first incident intake from the phone. EHS paged on severity threshold.
marketing.teams.site-managers.pain-point: Daily logs on a clipboard, transcribed at wrap
Photos on three different phones, never collected
Inspection sign-offs lost between the binder and the spreadsheet
marketing.teams.site-managers.faqs.0.q: Does it work when the site has no signal?
marketing.teams.site-managers.faqs.0.a: Yes. COMPVSS is offline-first. Punches, photos, and log entries queue on the phone and sync when coverage comes back. A generator field with zero bars still gets documented.
marketing.teams.site-managers.faqs.1.q: Who can close out an incident report?
marketing.teams.site-managers.faqs.1.a: Anyone on site can file one from the phone, anonymously if they want. Closing it is a manager sign-off. That split is enforced by role, so nothing quietly disappears between the field and the file.
marketing.teams.site-managers.faqs.2.q: Will the AHJ accept the inspection output?
marketing.teams.site-managers.faqs.2.a: The templates render to a signed PDF with pass/fail per item, photo evidence, and the inspector's sign-off timestamp. That's the format fire marshals and building officials ask for.
marketing.teams.technical-directors.blur: Tech ownership across audio, lighting, video, scenic, from specs through show-day.
marketing.teams.technical-directors.hero: Tech Specs Live On The Record.
TDs own the technical spine: system design, gear list, power, rigging, signal flow. ATLVS captures specs as typed records that survive into the season, the next show, and the post-mortem.
marketing.teams.technical-directors.work: Every asset tagged with status across its lifecycle. Cross-season availability view.
Source, return date, associated PO. Late returns flag in procurement.
Point loads, dynamic loads, motor calcs documented per show.
Circuit-by-circuit allocation with margin. Surfaces during inspection.
Shop work with cost, timeline, delivery photos. Variance to budget tracked.
Damage reports with photos and cost estimates. Bills automatically to sub or client.
marketing.teams.technical-directors.pain: Gear list lives in three spreadsheets
Sub-rental returns chased by text 3 weeks after wrap
Damage write-offs never reconciled to the client
marketing.teams.technical-directors.faqs: Does the registry handle both serialized units and bulk stock?
Yes. A moving-light with a serial number and a case of 200 shackles live in the same registry. Serialized assets track individually; lot assets carry a quantity and draw down as they're issued.
Can crew check gear out themselves?
Crew scan custody in and out from their own phones. Changing an asset's disposition, writing one off, or billing damage stays with the manager band. Scanning is open; the ledger is gated.
What happens when a sub-rental comes back late?
The return date lives on the rental record with its PO. Past-due returns flag in procurement instead of surfacing three weeks after wrap in a text thread.
marketing.teams.talent-buyers.blurb: Lead-to-booking pipeline: offers, contracts, advancing handoff.
marketing.teams.talent-buyers.hero.title: From Offer To Advancing. One Record.
marketing.teams.talent-buyers.hero.body: Talent buying sits between the sales pipeline and production. ATLVS keeps offers, contracts, and the handoff to advancing on one record so the artist team never asks 'who is on it?'.
Lead → offer → contract → confirmed → advancing. Per-show win rate and time-to-close.
23 block types signed in place. IP + timestamp captured. Stripe Connect deposit on accept.
Templated agreements with variable fees, riders, options.
Artists see their offer, rider, hospitality, set time. One link.
Confirmed offer flips to a project with advancing deliverables auto-created.
Final fees, back-end, deductions. Settled at the venue from live data.
marketing.teams.talent-buyers.pain-point: Offers in DocuSign, advancing in email, contracts in a folder
Re-entering the same artist + venue data three times
Handoff to production is a Slack message
marketing.teams.talent-buyers.faqs.0.q: What are the default deposit terms on a booking?
marketing.teams.talent-buyers.faqs.0.a: Offers default to a 60% deposit with the balance due at load-in. You can change it per offer, but the default matches how most talent deals actually settle.
marketing.teams.talent-buyers.faqs.1.q: Does the agent or manager need an account to sign?
marketing.teams.talent-buyers.faqs.1.a: No. The offer letter opens from a link and signs in place. IP and timestamp are captured on signature, and the deposit can collect on accept through Stripe.
marketing.teams.talent-buyers.faqs.2.q: What happens after an offer confirms?
marketing.teams.talent-buyers.faqs.2.a: The confirmed offer flips into a project with its advancing deliverables auto-created. Production picks up the record you already built instead of a forwarded email.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.blurb: Safety + medical + crisis ownership: incidents, OSHA, daily briefings.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.hero.title: Safety Isn't A Binder. It's A System.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.hero.body: EHS leads own incidents, OSHA logs, medical triage, daily briefings, crisis comms, BC/DR, safeguarding, environmental. ATLVS makes each a first-class record on the same database the show runs on.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.workflows.0.bo: From the phone, anonymous-capable. Photos, location, witnesses. Routes to EHS instantly.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.workflows.1.bo: Recordables flow from incidents. 300A summary one click before audit.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.workflows.2.bo: Per-day briefing with hazards, weather, PPE, comms, emergency assembly. Roster sign-on captured.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.workflows.3.bo: Ten built-in templates with show-ready gate.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.workflows.4.bo: Pre-approved templates publish in one tap.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.workflows.5.bo: Separately-scoped records with stricter access and audit.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.pain-points.0: Incidents on a clipboard, transcribed Monday morning
marketing.teams.hse-leads.pain-points.1: OSHA 300A assembled from emails three weeks before audit
marketing.teams.hse-leads.pain-points.2: Crisis comms drafted in real-time at 2am
marketing.teams.hse-leads.faqs.0.a: Yes. The field intake works with or without a name attached. Anonymous filings route to EHS the same way, with photos and location intact. You get the report you'd otherwise never hear about.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.faqs.1.q: Who is allowed to close an incident?
marketing.teams.hse-leads.faqs.1.a: Filing is open to everyone on site. Closing is a manager-band sign-off, enforced by role. The person who reported a hazard can't be the one who quietly marks it resolved.
marketing.teams.hse-leads.faqs.2.q: How does the OSHA log stay current?
marketing.teams.hse-leads.faqs.2.a: Recordables flow from the incident record as they're classified, so the 300 log builds during the season. The 300A summary exports in one click when the audit letter arrives.
marketing.teams.production-coordinator.b: The production office's connective tissue. Tasks, calls, travel, paperwork, and the answer to every 'who has that?'
marketing.teams.production-coordinator.h: Right now Monday is forty unread threads asking where things stand. On ATLVS the task list, the schedule, and the travel grid are the same record everyone else is reading, so the answer to 'where does that live?' becomes one link.
marketing.teams.production-coordinator.w: One personal spine: your open tasks, pending approvals, requests you filed. Everything with your name on it, in one list on your phone.
Assign, date, done. Tasks live on the project record, so 'did that happen?' has an answer without a status meeting.
Calls flow from the schedule to each person's portal and calendar. When the call moves, the notification goes out; you don't re-text thirty people.
Flights, ground, hotel blocks, roommate pairs. Each person sees their own itinerary in COMPVSS instead of asking you for the confirmation number again.
Gear, purchase reqs, time off, IT, report-it. Five structured intakes replace the 'hey, quick favor' texts. You file it; the approval routes itself.
The packet you built last show becomes the template for the next one. Field forms and documents live where the crew fills them in.
marketing.teams.production-coordinator.p: The itinerary lives in your inbox and everyone knows it
Approvals stall because nobody knows whose desk they're on
The same call time gets texted, emailed, and posted, then changes
Every show starts with rebuilding last show's paperwork
marketing.teams.production-coordinator.f: How is this different from a general task app?
The tasks sit on production records. A task attached to a load-in, a requisition, or an artist advance carries its context with it, so closing it updates the thing it was actually about.
Can I approve things, or just file them?

Depends on your role. Coordinators typically file requests and route them; approvals sit with the manager band. That split is enforced by permissions, which means an approval on the record is a real approval.
Does travel really live here too?
Yes. Travel and lodging are assignment records per person per project. Each traveler sees their own legs and room in the field app; you see the whole grid.
marketing.teams.crew-freelancer.blurb: The people who build the show. Call times, credentials, hours, and getting paid without chasing anyone.
marketing.teams.crew-freelancer.hero.bod: Before: call time in one text, parking in another, your W-9 in an email from March. After: COMPVSS on your phone has the shift, the gate, your credential, and your hours. It keeps working with zero bars.
marketing.teams.crew-freelancer.workflow: Punch in from the phone with geo verification against the site zone. Your hours are yours, on the record, from the first punch.
See your shifts, request a swap, get the answer in the app. No group-chat archaeology to figure out if you work Saturday.
Credential, radio, meal, ticket, uniform. Everything issued to you for the show, listed with its state, so the gate conversation is short.
Timesheets build from your punches. Receipts photograph straight into an expense. Mileage logs from the phone. Payday math stops being your problem.
Request it in the app, watch it route, get a decision. The approval lives on the record instead of in someone's memory.
Open gigs and calls post to the public marketplace. Your profile and availability calendar do the pitching while you're on a show.
marketing.teams.crew-freelancer.pain-poi: Call time changes and you find out at the gate
Hours disputed three weeks later with no record
Receipts in a jacket pocket until they're unreadable
Every new gig means a new app, a new login, a new W-9
marketing.teams.crew-freelancer.faqs.0.a: Two ways. If a production hires you, they send an invite or an org code and you land in COMPVSS on your phone. If you're starting your own outfit, organizations are created in LEG3ND on the web, and then you invite your crew.
marketing.teams.crew-freelancer.faqs.1.a: No. COMPVSS is built phone-first and works offline. Punches, photos, and filings queue on the device and sync when you're back in coverage.
marketing.teams.crew-freelancer.faqs.2.q: Who can see my hours and documents?
marketing.teams.crew-freelancer.faqs.2.a: You see your own records; your personal documents stay yours. Timesheets are visible to you and the managers who approve them. Access is enforced at the database layer per role, so a fellow crew member can't browse your file.
marketing.teams.crew-freelancer.faqs.3.q: Can I work for more than one company?
marketing.teams.crew-freelancer.faqs.3.a: Yes. One login, multiple workspaces. Each production's data stays inside its own org; switching shows takes two taps.
Warehouse & Asset Managers
Owner of the shop and everything in it. The registry, the scans, the custody chain, the truck that leaves at 6am.
For Warehouse & Asset Managers
Every Case, Accounted For.

The truck leaves at 6 and you find out at 8 what didn't make it. On ATLVS a case that leaves the shop is a scan, and the scan is custody. What's out, who has it, and when it's due back stop being questions.
One Asset Registry
Gear, fleet, and lot stock in a single store with class, quantity, and disposition. The spreadsheet with the 'FINAL_v3' filename retires.
Scan Custody
QR and barcode scans from any phone move custody in and out. The chain of who touched what survives load-out, the show, and the 2am reload.
Master Catalog
Every SKU your org issues, from radios to wristbands, in one catalog. Assignments draw from it, so counts reconcile instead of drifting.
Kit Fulfillment
Radios, tools, and uniforms issue per person per show and come back through the same lifecycle. Issued, transferred, returned. No orphan gear.
Runs + Logistics
Vehicle runs with driver, manifest, and proof of delivery. The field app shows the driver their run; you see the board.
Damage, Billed
Damage reports carry photos and a cost estimate, and bill to the sub or the client instead of dying in a wrap-week email.
The count is right until the first truck loads
Custody is a memory, so losses are a mystery
Sub-rental returns tracked in a text thread
Write-offs discovered at year-end, not at wrap
What scanning hardware do I need?
None to start. QR and barcodes scan with the phone camera in COMPVSS. If you run RFID, wedge scanners that emulate a keyboard work with the same intake.
Can crew check gear out themselves?
Yes, and that's the point: crew scan custody at the case. Changing a disposition, writing an asset off, or billing damage stays with the manager band, enforced by role. Open scanning, gated ledger.
How do quantities work for bulk stock?
Lot assets carry a quantity and draw down as they issue; serialized assets track one by one. Both roll up in the same inventory view, so the shackle count and the console serial live on one screen.
marketing.teams.finance-controller.blurb: The one who closes the month. Capture at the source, approvals with teeth, terms that collect themselves.
Close The Month Without The Shoebox.

Today the month closes on receipts photographed in four different apps. On ATLVS the field captures its own paper: expenses, mileage, and hours land coded from the crew's phones, and you approve from yours. The full console ledger is coming; the capture layer works now.
marketing.teams.finance-controller.workf: Crew photograph the receipt into a coded expense the day they spend it. You stop reconstructing a show from a pile at wrap.
Mileage files from the phone against the project. Rate math is done for you, and the log survives an audit.
Timesheets build from geo-verified clock punches, so the labor number you post is the labor that happened on site.
A requisition filed in the field spends nothing until the controller band signs. Approval chains are role-gated, and the decision lives on the record.
Proposals carry a 50/50 deposit split by default; talent bookings run 60/40 with the balance at load-in. Signed online, deposits collect through Stripe on acceptance.
Budgets, AP and AR, and settlement in the operator console are in build. We'd rather tell you that than sell you a screenshot.
marketing.teams.finance-controller.pain-: Labor actuals arrive two pay cycles late
POs approved verbally, disputed in writing
Deposit terms renegotiated by accident in email
What's live today and what's still coming?

Live now: field capture of expenses, mileage, and timesheets, role-gated approvals from the phone, proposals with deposit terms that sign and collect online, and vendor payouts through Stripe Connect. In build: the full console ledger with budgets, AP/AR, and settlement views.
Vendors onboard a Stripe Connect account through their portal. Payouts route on approval as ACH, card, or wire, and every payout ties back to its PO.
No. Finance surfaces are role-gated at the database layer. Crew see their own expenses, hours, and reimbursements; budget and margin stay with the roles you grant.
marketing.teams.marketing-content-lead.b: The voice of the show. Guides, ticket pages, sponsor delivery, and one update that lands everywhere.
marketing.teams.marketing-content-lead.h: Show week you're pasting the same update into an email, a doc, and three chats. On ATLVS you publish once and each audience reads its own version: the guest guide, the crew feed, the sponsor portal, the ticket page. Same record underneath.
marketing.teams.marketing-content-lead.w: One guide, scoped per persona. Guests get parking and doors; crew get radio channels and PPE; artists get their set time. Sixteen section types, one source.
Tiers, transfers, and capacity read from the same records the gate scans against, so the storefront never advertises a tier that sold out yesterday.
Each sponsor's portal carries their activation specs, asset library, and entitlements, plus reporting on what actually got delivered. Renewal conversations start from evidence.
Post to the feed and it reaches every phone on the crew, with read counts. The 'nobody told me' era ends quietly.
Site photos collect per project with dates and locations intact. The recap deck builds from the gallery instead of a plea in the group chat.
Deeper campaign and content tooling in the operator console is in build. What's above ships today.
marketing.teams.marketing-content-lead.p: Five versions of the event info, four of them stale
Sponsor recap assembled by hand the week after wrap
The good photos live on someone's phone
Guest questions answered one DM at a time
marketing.teams.marketing-content-lead.f: Do guests need to install anything?
No. The guest guide and ticket surfaces open from a link in any browser. COMPVSS, the installable app, is for your crew.
Who can publish, and who can only draft?
Publishing is a manager-band permission. Anyone you grant access can draft; the update goes live when a publisher pushes it. Drafts never leak to guests or sponsors.
Can the guest-facing surfaces carry our brand?
Yes, on Enterprise: custom branding and custom domains on the portal. Most promoters white-label the guest side and keep the operator side stock.
marketing.teams.vendor-subcontractor.blu: The outside shop the show depends on. POs in writing, compliance in one place, payouts without the phone calls.
marketing.teams.vendor-subcontractor.her: Get The PO. Do The Work. Get Paid.

You've chased a net-60 check with three phone calls before. The vendor portal your client sends you shows the PO, the compliance docs it needs, and where your payout stands, in one login. No seat license, no software to buy.
marketing.teams.vendor-subcontractor.wor: COI and W-9 upload straight into the portal, with expiry tracked. A current certificate is the key that unlocks POs, so the paperwork conversation happens once.
The order, its line items, and its terms live in your portal. Verbal scope changes stop being your problem to prove.
Producers publish RFQs to the public marketplace. You respond through the portal with your quals attached, against stated requirements instead of a guessing game.
Onboard a Stripe Connect account and payouts land on PO fulfillment as ACH, card, or wire. Each payout ties to its PO, so reconciliation on your side is a lookup.
Your portal carries a message thread to your account manager on the production side. Questions land with the person who can answer them.
Each producer you work with sends their own portal; your login carries across. Their worlds stay separate, your inbox stays sane.
marketing.teams.vendor-subcontractor.pai: Scope agreed on a call, disputed at invoice
COI expires mid-show and nobody caught it
Payment status is a weekly phone call
Five clients, five spreadsheets of your own paperwork
marketing.teams.vendor-subcontractor.faq: What does the portal cost me?
Nothing. Your client's organization runs the platform; you're invited in. You get the PO record, compliance vault, messaging, and payout status without buying a seat.
Through Stripe Connect. You onboard a payout account once, and approved POs pay out directly as ACH, card, or wire. No invoice-into-the-void step.
Can my client see my other work?
No. Each portal is scoped to that organization's projects, enforced at the database layer. Client A cannot see that Client B exists, and neither sees anything of yours beyond what you submit to them.
What happens if my insurance lapses?
The portal flags the expiry ahead of time, and new POs gate until a current COI is on file. Annoying by design; it also means you never lose a job over paperwork you didn't know was stale.
pdf.aiaPayApp.title: Pay Application #${String(payApp.application_number).padStart(4, "0")}
pdf.aiaPayApp.contractorCertificationBod: The undersigned Contractor certifies that to the best of the Contractor's knowledge, information, and belief the Work covered by this Application for Payment has been completed in accordance with the Contract Documents, that all amounts have been paid by the Contractor for Work for which previous Certificates for Payment were issued and payments received from the Owner, and that current payment shown herein is now due.
pdf.aiaPayApp.architectCertificateBody: In accordance with the Contract Documents, based on on-site observations and the data comprising this Application, the Architect certifies to the Owner that to the best of the Architect's knowledge, information, and belief the Work has progressed as indicated, the quality of the Work is in accordance with the Contract Documents, and the Contractor is entitled to payment of the AMOUNT CERTIFIED.
pdf.aiaPayApp.columnsNote: Columns A–I per AIA G703. Amounts in USD.
pdf.brandKit.title: Brand kit
pdf.brandKit.coverSubtitle: Design tokens, logo, and style rules.
pdf.brandKit.bodySample: Body 11pt, 1.45 line-height. Use Helvetica for interoperability in field prints.
pdf.brandKit.usageRule1: • Use the accent color for running header bars + primary CTAs.
pdf.brandKit.usageRule2: • Tier 4+ documents carry a crimson classification banner — override the accent only for the banner.
pdf.brandKit.usageRule3: • Legal footer appears on every page of every producer-generated document.
pdf.certifiedPayroll.formWh347: U.S. DOL Form WH-347 — Davis-Bacon Certified Payroll
pdf.certifiedPayroll.title: Certified Payroll
pdf.certifiedPayroll.hoursColumnsNote: Hours columns are Sun / Mon / Tue / Wed / Thu / Fri / Sat. Amounts in USD.
pdf.certifiedPayroll.clause1: (1) That I pay or supervise the payment of the persons employed by ${contractor.name} on the ${project.name} project; that during the payroll period commencing on the ${fmtDate(payroll.pay_period_start)} day and ending the ${fmtDate(payroll.pay_period_end)} day, all persons employed on said project have been paid the full weekly wages earned, that no rebates have been or will be made either directly or indirectly to or on behalf of said contractor or subcontractor from the full weekly wages earned by any person and that no deductions have been made either directly or indirectly from the full wages earned by any person, other than permissible deductions as defined in Regulations, Part 3 (29 CFR Subtitle A).
pdf.certifiedPayroll.clause2: (2) That any payrolls otherwise under this contract required to be submitted for the above period are correct and complete; that the wage rates for laborers or mechanics contained therein are not less than the applicable wage rates contained in any wage determination incorporated into the contract; that the classifications set forth therein for each laborer or mechanic conform with the work he performed.
pdf.certifiedPayroll.clause3: (3) That any apprentices employed in the above period are duly registered in a bona fide apprenticeship program registered with a State apprenticeship agency recognized by the Bureau of Apprenticeship and Training, United States Department of Labor, or if no such recognized agency exists in a State, are registered with the Bureau of Apprenticeship and Training, United States Department of Labor.
pdf.certifiedPayroll.clause4WithFringes: (4) That fringe benefits required by the wage determination have been paid as applicable — total fringe payments this period: ${money(payroll.total_fringes)}.
pdf.certifiedPayroll.clause4NoFringes: (4) That fringe benefits required by the wage determination have been paid as applicable — no fringes due this period.

pdf.wipReport.tableNote: All amounts in USD. % Comp is by cost. Over/Under = Earned − Billed (positive = over-billed).
pdf.wipReport.methodologyBody: Percent-complete = costs_to_date / estimated_at_completion. Earned revenue = revised_contract × % comp. Over/under-billed = earned − billed (positive = over-billed, negative = under-billed). Estimated cost-to-complete = EAC − costs_to_date.
